sections.label: Section 1.1
sections.translation: Golden lyre , rightful joint possession of Apollo and the violet-haired Muses , to which the dance-step listens , the beginning of splendid festivity ; and singers obey your notes , whenever , with your quivering strings , you prepare to strike up chorus-leading preludes .

sections.label: Section 1.5
sections.translation: You quench even the warlike thunderbolt of everlasting fire . And the eagle sleeps on the scepter of Zeus , relaxing his swift wings on either side ,

sections.label: Section 1.7
sections.original: ἀρχὸς οἰωνῶν , κελαινῶπιν δʼ ἐπί οἱ νεφέλαν
sections.translation: the king of birds ; and you pour down a dark mist over his curved head , a sweet seal on his eyelids . Slumbering , he ripples his liquid back ,

sections.label: Section 1.10
sections.original: ῥιπαῖσι κατασχόμενος . καὶ γὰρ βιατὰς Ἄρης , τραχεῖαν ἄνευθε λιπὼν
sections.translation: under the spell of your pulsing notes . Even powerful Ares , setting aside the rough spear-point , warms his heart in repose ; your shafts charm the minds even of the gods , by virtue of the skill of Leto ’ s son and the deep-bosomed Muses .

sections.label: Section 1.13
sections.original: ὅσσα δὲ μὴ πεφίληκε Ζεύς , ἀτύζονται βοὰν
sections.translation: But those whom Zeus does not love are stunned with terror when they hear the cry of the Pierian Muses , on earth or on the irresistible sea ;

sections.label: Section 1.15
sections.original: 30 ὅς τʼ ἐν αἰνᾷ Ταρτάρῳ κεῖται , θεῶν πολέμιος ,
sections.translation: among them is he who lies in dread Tartarus , that enemy of the gods , Typhon with his hundred heads . Once the famous Cilician cave nurtured him , but now the sea-girt cliffs above Cumae , and Sicily too , lie heavy on his shaggy chest . And the pillar of the sky holds him down ,

sections.label: Section 1.20
sections.original: νιφόεσσʼ Αἴτνα , πάνετες χιόνος ὀξείας τιθήνα ·
sections.translation: snow-covered Aetna , year-round nurse of bitter frost ,

sections.label: Section 1.21
sections.original: 40 τᾶς ἐρεύγονται μὲν ἀπλάτου πυρὸς ἁγνόταται
sections.translation: from whose inmost caves belch forth the purest streams of unapproachable fire . In the daytime her rivers roll out a fiery flood of smoke , while in the darkness of night the crimson flame hurls rocks down to the deep plain of the sea with a crashing roar .

sections.label: Section 1.25
sections.translation: That monster shoots up the most terrible jets of fire ; it is a marvellous wonder to see , and a marvel even to hear about when men are present .

sections.label: Section 1.27
sections.original: οἷον Αἴτνας ἐν μελαμφύλλοις δέδεται κορυφαῖς
sections.translation: Such a creature is bound beneath the dark and leafy heights of Aetna and beneath the plain , and his bed scratches and goads the whole length of his back stretched out against it . Grant that we may be pleasing to you , Zeus ,

sections.label: Section 1.30
sections.original: ὃς τοῦτʼ ἐφέπεις ὄρος , εὐκάρποιο γαίας μέτωπον , τοῦ μὲν ἐπωνυμίαν
sections.translation: you who frequent this mountain , this brow of the fruitful earth , whose namesake city near at hand was glorified by its renowned founder , when the herald at the Pythian racecourse proclaimed the name of Aetna , announcing Hieron ’ s triumph

sections.label: Section 2.1
sections.translation: [*] Great city of Syracuse ! Sacred precinct of Ares , plunged deep in war ! Divine nurse of men and horses who rejoice in steel ! For you I come from splendid Thebes bringing this song , a message of the earth-shaking four-horse race

sections.label: Section 2.5
sections.translation: in which Hieron with his fine chariot won the victory , and so crowned Ortygia with far-shining garlands — Ortygia , home of Artemis the river-goddess : not without her help did Hieron master with his gentle hands the horses with embroidered reins .

sections.label: Section 2.9
sections.original: ἐπὶ γὰρ ἰοχέαιρα παρθένος χερὶ διδύμᾳ
sections.translation: For the virgin goddess who showers arrows

sections.label: Section 2.10
sections.original: 20 ὅ τʼ ἐναγώνιος Ἑρμᾶς αἰγλᾶντα τίθησι κόσμον , ξεστὸν ὅταν δίφρον
sections.translation: and Hermes the god of contests present the gleaming reins to him with both hands when he yokes the strength of his horses to the polished car , to the chariot that obeys the bit , and calls on the wide-ruling god who wields the trident . Other kings have other men to pay them the tribute of melodious song , the recompense for excellence .

sections.label: Section 2.15
sections.translation: The voices of the men of Cyprus often shout the name of Cinyras , whom golden-haired Apollo gladly loved ,

sections.label: Section 2.17
sections.original: ἱερέα κτίλον Ἀφροδίτας · ἄγει δὲ χάρις φίλων ποίνιμος ἀντὶ ἔργων ὀπιζομένα ·
sections.translation: Cinyras , the obedient priest of Aphrodite . Reverent gratitude is a recompense for friendly deeds . And you , son of Deinomenes , the West Locrian girl invokes you , standing outside her door : out of the helpless troubles of war ,

sections.label: Section 2.20
sections.translation: through your power she looks at the world in security . They say that by the commands of the gods Ixion spins round and round on his feathered wheel , saying this to mortals : “ Repay your benefactor frequently with gentle favors in return .”

sections.label: Section 2.25
sections.original: ἔμαθε δὲ σαφές . εὐμενέσσι γὰρ παρὰ Κρονίδαις
sections.translation: He learned a clear lesson . For although he received a sweet life among the gracious children of Cronus , he did not abide his prosperity for long , when in his madness of spirit he desired Hera , who was allotted to the joyful bed of Zeus . But his arrogance drove him to extreme delusion ; and soon the man suffered a suitable

sections.label: Section 2.30
sections.original: ἐξαίρετον ἕλε μόχθον . αἱ δύο δʼ ἀμπλακίαι
sections.translation: exquisite punishment . Both of his crimes brought him toil in the end . First , he was the hero who , not without guile , was the first to stain mortal men with kindred blood ;

sections.label: Section 3.1
sections.translation: If it were proper for this commonplace prayer to be made by my tongue , I would want Cheiron the son of Philyra to be alive again , he who has departed , the wide-ruling son of Cronus son of Uranus ; and I would want him to reign again in the glens of Pelion , the beast of the wilds

sections.label: Section 3.5
sections.original: 10 νοῦν ἔχοντʼ ἀνδρῶν φίλον · οἷος ἐὼν θρέψεν ποτὲ
sections.translation: whose mind was friendly to men ; just as he was when once he reared Asclepius , that gentle craftsman who drove pain from the limbs that he healed , that hero who cured all types of diseases .

sections.label: Section 3.8
sections.translation: His mother , the daughter of Phlegyas with his fine horses , before she could bring him to term with the help of Eleithuia who attends on childbirth , was stricken by the golden

sections.label: Section 3.10
sections.translation: arrows of Artemis in her bedroom and descended to the house of Hades , by the skills of Apollo . The anger of the children of Zeus is not in vain . But she made light of Apollo , in the error of her mind , and consented to another marriage without her father ’ s knowledge , although she had before lain with Phoebus of the unshorn hair ,

sections.label: Section 3.15
sections.translation: and was bearing within her the pure seed of the god . She did not wait for the marriage-feast to come , nor for the full-voiced cry of the hymenaeal chorus , such things as unmarried girls her own age love to murmur in evening songs to their companion . [*] Instead ,

sections.label: Section 3.20
sections.original: ἤρατο τῶν ἀπεόντων · οἷα καὶ πολλοὶ πάθον .
sections.translation: she was in love with what was distant ; many others have felt that passion . There is a worthless tribe among men which dishonors what is at home and looks far away , hunting down empty air with hopes that cannot be fulfilled .

sections.label: Section 3.24
sections.translation: Such was the strong infatuation

sections.label: Section 3.25
sections.original: καλλιπέπλου λῆμα Κορωνίδος . ἐλθόντος γὰρ εὐνάσθη ξένου
sections.translation: that the spirit of lovely-robed Coronis had caught . For she lay in the bed of a stranger who came from Arcadia ; but she did not elude the watcher . Even in Pytho where sheep are sacrificed , the king of the temple happened to perceive it , Loxias , persuading his thoughts with his unerring counsellor : his mind , which knows all things . He does not grasp falsehood , and he is deceived

sections.label: Section 3.30
sections.original: οὐ θεὸς οὐ βροτὸς ἔργοις οὔτε βουλαῖς .
sections.translation: by neither god nor man , neither in deeds nor in thoughts .

sections.label: Section 4.1
sections.original: σάμερον μὲν χρή σε παρʼ ἀνδρὶ φίλῳ
sections.translation: Today you must stand beside a beloved man , Muse , the king of Cyrene with its fine horses , so that while Arcesilas celebrates his triumph you may swell the fair wind of song that is due to the children of Leto and to Pytho , where once the priestess seated beside the golden eagles of Zeus ,

sections.label: Section 4.5
sections.original: οὐκ ἀποδάμου Ἀπόλλωνος τυχόντος , ἱέ ρεα
sections.translation: on a day when Apollo happened to be present , gave an oracle naming Battus as the colonizer of fruitful Libya , and telling how he would at once leave the holy island and found a city of fine chariots on a shining white breast of the earth ,

sections.label: Section 4.9
sections.translation: and carry out

sections.label: Section 4.10
sections.original: ἑβδόμᾳ καὶ σὺν δεκάτᾳ γενεᾷ Θήραιον , Αἰήτα τό ποτε ζαμενὴς
sections.translation: in the seventeenth generation the word spoken at Thera by Medea , which once the inspired daughter of Aeetes , the queen of the Colchians , breathed forth from her immortal mouth . She spoke in this way to the heroes who sailed with the warrior Jason : “ Hear me , sons of high-spirited men and of gods . For I say that from this wave-washed land one day the daughter of Epaphus

sections.label: Section 4.15
sections.translation: will have planted in her a root of cities that are [*] dear to men , in the temple of Zeus Ammon .

sections.label: Section 4.17
sections.original: 30 ἀντὶ δελφίνων δʼ ἐλαχυπτερύγων ἵππους ἀμείψαντες θοάς ,
sections.translation: Instead of short-finned dolphins they will have swift horses , and reins instead of oars , and they will drive storm-footed chariot teams . That token shall make

sections.label: Section 4.20
sections.original: ματρόπολιν Θήραν γενέσθαι , τόν ποτε Τριτωνίδος ἐν προχοαῖς
sections.translation: Thera the mother-city of great cities , the token which once , beside the out-flowing waters of lake Tritonis , Euphemus received as he descended from the prow , a clod of earth as a gift of friendship from a god in the likeness of a man . And as a sign of favor , Zeus the son of Cronus sounded a peal of thunder ,

sections.label: Section 4.24
sections.translation: when the stranger found us hanging the bronze-jawed anchor

sections.label: Section 4.25
sections.original: ναῒ κρημνάντων ἐπέτοσσε , θοᾶς Ἀργοῦς χαλινόν . δώδεκα δὲ πρότερον
sections.translation: , the bridle of the swift Argo , against the ship . Before that we had been dragging our seafaring ship for twelve days from the Ocean over the deserted back of the land , having drawn it ashore by my counsels . And then the solitary god approached , who had assumed the splendid appearance of an honored man . He began to speak friendly words ,

sections.label: Section 4.30
sections.translation: such as beneficent hosts use when they first invite arriving strangers to a meal .

sections.label: Section 5.1
sections.translation: Wealth is widely powerful , whenever a mortal man receives it , blended with pure excellence , from the hands of fortune , and takes it as a companion that makes many friends .

sections.label: Section 5.5
sections.translation: Arcesilas , favored by the gods , from the first steps of your famous life you seek for it with glory , by the grace of Castor with his golden chariot ,

sections.label: Section 5.10
sections.original: εὐδίαν ὃς μετὰ χειμέριον ὄμβρον τεὰν
sections.translation: who , after the wintry storm , sheds calm on your blessed hearth .

sections.label: Section 5.12
sections.translation: Skillful men are better able to bear even god-given power . Great prosperity surrounds you , as you walk with justice .

sections.label: Section 5.15
sections.translation: First , since you are a king of great cities , your inborn eye looks on this as a most revered prize of honor , united with your mind ;

sections.label: Section 5.20
sections.original: μάκαρ δὲ καὶ νῦν , κλεεννᾶς ὅτι
sections.translation: and you are blessed even now , because you have already earned the boast of victory with your horses from the renowned Pythian festival , and you will welcome this victory-procession of men ,

sections.label: Section 5.23
sections.original: 30 Ἀπολλώνιον ἄθυρμα . τῷ σε μὴ λαθέτω
sections.translation: a delight for Apollo . And so , do not forget , when you are celebrated in song around Cyrene ’ s sweet garden of Aphrodite ,

sections.label: Section 5.25
sections.translation: to set the god in the highest place as the cause of all things , and to love Carrhotus above all your companions . He did not bring with him Excuse , the daughter of late-thinking Afterthought , when he came to the house of the descendants of Battus who rule by right ;

sections.label: Section 5.30
sections.translation: but he was welcomed beside the waters of Castalia , and he flung over your hair the prize of honor for the victorious chariot ;

sections.label: Section 6.1
sections.translation: Listen ! for we are again ploughing the field of dark-eyed Aphrodite , or of the Graces , as we approach the sacred navel of the loud-roaring land ;

sections.label: Section 6.5
sections.translation: where , for the prosperous Emmenids and Acragas on the river , and especially for Xenocrates , a Pythian victor ’ s treasure-house of songs has been built and is ready in the glen of Apollo , rich in gold .

sections.label: Section 6.10
sections.original: τὸν οὔτε χειμέριος ὄμβρος ἐπακτὸς ἐλθών ,
sections.translation: It is buffeted by neither the invading onset of winter rain , the loud-roaring cloud ’ s pitiless army , nor the wind that sweeps all kinds of rubble into the depths of the sea . Its facade , shining in pure light ,

sections.label: Section 6.15
sections.original: πατρὶ τεῷ , Θρασύβουλε , κοινάν τε γενεᾷ
sections.translation: will announce your chariot victory to the speech of men and make it famous — the victory you share with your father and your race , Thrasybulus , won in the vales of Crisa .

sections.label: Section 6.19
sections.original: σύ τοι σχεθών νιν ἐπιδέξια χειρός , ὀρθὰν
sections.translation: You keep it on your right hand and

sections.label: Section 6.20
sections.translation: uphold the commandment , one of the precepts which they say once in the mountains the son of Philyra enjoined on the powerful son of Peleus , when he was separated from his parents : first of the gods , worship the son of Cronus , the loud-voiced ruler of lightning and thunder ;

sections.label: Section 6.25
sections.translation: and never deprive your parents of such honor during their allotted lifetime .

sections.label: Section 6.28
sections.translation: Long ago , too , powerful Antilochus showed that he had this way of thinking ;

sections.label: Section 6.30
sections.translation: he died for his father ’ s sake , by awaiting the man-slaying commander of the Ethiopians , Memnon . For the horse kept Nestor ’ s chariot from moving , since it had been wounded by Paris ’ arrows ; and Memnon was aiming his strong spear .

sections.label: Section 7.1
sections.translation: The great city of Athens is the most beautiful prelude of song , which the widely powerful race of the Alcmaeonids can lay as a foundation of odes in honor of their horses .

sections.label: Section 7.5
sections.translation: What fatherland , what family will you name that is more illustrious in Greece ?

sections.label: Section 7.9
sections.translation: For in all cities the story
